From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Asian literature** is the literature produced in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").

## Classical Chinese and Japanese literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asian_literature&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classical Chinese and Japanese literature")]

Main articles: [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature") and [Japanese literature](/wiki/Japanese_literature "Japanese literature")

In [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") and [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") [China](/wiki/China "China"), famous [poets](/wiki/Poet "Poet") such as [Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai") authored works of great importance. They wrote *shī* ([Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese"): 詩) poems, which have lines with equal numbers of [characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character"), as well as *cí* (詞) poems with mixed line varieties. [Early-Modern Japanese literature](/wiki/Japanese_literature#Early-modern_literature "Japanese literature") (17th–19th centuries) developed comparable innovations such as [haiku](/wiki/Haiku "Haiku"), a form of [Japanese poetry](/wiki/Japanese_poetry "Japanese poetry") that evolved from the ancient *[hokku](/wiki/Hokku "Hokku")* ([Japanese language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 発句) mode. Haiku consists of three sections (all in a single vertical line in Japanese): the first and third segments each have five *morae* (which are not the [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") equivalent of [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable")), while the second has seven. Original haiku masters included such figures as [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") poet [Matsuo Bashō](/wiki/Matsuo_Bash%C5%8D "Matsuo Bashō") (松尾芭蕉); others influenced by Bashō include [Kobayashi Issa](/wiki/Kobayashi_Issa "Kobayashi Issa") and [Masaoka Shiki](/wiki/Masaoka_Shiki "Masaoka Shiki").

## Classical Indian literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asian_literature&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classical Indian literature")]

Main article: [Indian literature](/wiki/Indian_literature "Indian literature")

## Classical West Asian literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asian_literature&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classical West Asian literature")]

Main articles: [Persian literature](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature"), [Arabic literature](/wiki/Arabic_literature "Arabic literature"), [Jewish literature](/wiki/Jewish_literature "Jewish literature"), and [Turkish literature](/wiki/Turkish_literature "Turkish literature")

## Modern Asian literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asian_literature&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern Asian literature")]

The [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore"), a [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_literature "Bengali literature") [poet](/wiki/Poet "Poet"), [dramatist](/wiki/Drama "Drama"), and [writer](/wiki/Writer "Writer") who was an [Indian](/wiki/India "India"), became in 1913 the first Asian [Nobel laureate](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"). He won his [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") for notable impact his prose works and poetic thought had on [English](/wiki/English_literature "English literature"), [French](/wiki/French_literature "French literature"), and other national literatures of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). He also wrote the [Indian anthem](/wiki/Indian_anthem "Indian anthem"). Later, other Asian writers won Nobel Prizes in literature, including [Yasunari Kawabata](/wiki/Yasunari_Kawabata "Yasunari Kawabata") ([Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), 1966), and [Kenzaburō Ōe](/wiki/Kenzabur%C5%8D_%C5%8Ce "Kenzaburō Ōe") ([Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), 1994). [Yasunari Kawabata](/wiki/Yasunari_Kawabata "Yasunari Kawabata") wrote novels
and short stories distinguished by their elegant and spartan diction such as the novels [Snow Country](/wiki/Snow_Country "Snow Country") and [The Master of Go](/wiki/The_Master_of_Go "The Master of Go").
